title: Two-sample z interval for the difference of proportions
description: Practice calculating a two-sample z interval to estimate the difference between two population proportions.
topic_path: Confidence intervals for the difference of two proportions
Two-sample z interval for the difference of proportions

assessment_items.question_content:
Julia wonders if people living in the eastern part of her country are more likely to respond favorably to a new advertisement than the people in the western part of her country. She randomly selects $100$ people from each of these parts of her country. She finds that $80$ people in the eastern part of the country respond favorably, while only $60$ people in the western part do.

Julia wants to use these results to construct a $95\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference in the proportion of all people who would respond favorably in these parts of her country $(\text{eastern} - \text{western})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $95\%$ confidence interval based on Julia's samples?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Our sample difference

$\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ are the proportions of successes from the two samples. 

In our example $\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ are the proportion of people who responded favorably in each part of the country — eastern and western, respectively. Let's calculate both sample proportions $\hat p_{\text{Eastern}}$ and $\hat p_{\text{Western}}$ first:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{\text{E}}&=\dfrac{80}{100}=\blueD{0.8} \\\\
\hat p_{\text{W}}&=\dfrac{60}{100}=\greenD{0.6}
\end{align}$

So the sample difference is $\hat{p}_{\text{E}}-\hat{p}_{\text{W}}=\blueD{0.8}-\greenD{0.6}$.

###Determining the critical value

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

##Determining the standard error

The standard error for the difference of two proportions is:

$\text{SE}_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}_1\left(1-\hat{p}_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat{p}_2\left(1-\hat{p}_2\right)}{n_2}}$

In this example, we use $\hat p_\text{E}=\blueD{0.8}$, $\hat p_\text{W}=\greenD{0.6}$, $n_\text{E}=\purpleD{100}$, and $n_\text{W}=\maroonD{100}$.

$\text{SE}_{\hat{p}_{\text{E}}-\hat{p}_{\text{W}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\blueD{0.8}(1-\blueD{0.8})}{\purpleD{100}}+\dfrac{\greenD{0.6}(1-\greenD{0.6})}{\maroonD{100}}}$

###Answer

The correct $95\%$ confidence interval is:

$(0.8-0.6)\pm1.96\sqrt{\dfrac{0.8(0.2)}{100}+\dfrac{0.6(0.4)}{100}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Melina works on the campaign of a political candidate. She suspected that voters in district $1$ were more likely to support her candidate than voters in district $2$. She obtained separate random samples of voters from each district. Here are the results:

Supports candidate? | District $1$ | District $2$
- | -: | -:
Yes | $64$ | $96$
No | $36$ | $104$
**Total** | $100$ | $200$

Melina wants to use these results to construct a $90\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference in the proportion of voters in these districts who support her candidate $(p_1-p_2)$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $90\%$ confidence interval based on Melina's samples?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Our sample difference

$\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ are the proportions of successes from the two samples. 

In our example $\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ are the proportion of people in each district who support Melina's candidate. Let's calculate both sample proportions $\hat p_1$ and $\hat p_2$ first:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_1&=\dfrac{64}{100}=\blueD{0.64} \\\\
\hat p_2&=\dfrac{96}{200}=\greenD{0.48}
\end{align}$

So the sample difference is $\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2=\blueD{0.64}-\greenD{0.48}$.

###Determining the critical value

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $90\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.645$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

##Determining the standard error

The standard error for the difference of two proportions is:

$\text{SE}_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}_1\left(1-\hat{p}_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat{p}_2\left(1-\hat{p}_2\right)}{n_2}}$

In this example, we use $\hat p_1=\blueD{0.64}$, $\hat p_2=\greenD{0.48}$, $n_1=\purpleD{100}$, and $n_2=\maroonD{200}$.

$\text{SE}_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\blueD{0.64}(1-\blueD{0.64})}{\purpleD{100}}+\dfrac{\greenD{0.48}(1-\greenD{0.48})}{\maroonD{200}}}$

###Answer

The correct $90\%$ confidence interval is:

$(0.64-0.48)\pm1.645\sqrt{\dfrac{0.64(0.36)}{100}+\dfrac{0.48(0.52)}{200}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Jaime is a market researcher interested in nutrition. He suspected that people in the $20\text{-}29$ age group were more likely to say they were vegetarian than people in the $30\text{-}39$ age group. He obtained separate random samples of people from each age group. Here are the results:

Vegetarian? | $20\text{-}29$ | $30\text{-}39$
- | -: | -:
Yes | $12$ | $12$
No | $68$ | $108$
**Total** | $80$ | $120$

Jaime wants to use these results to construct a $99\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference between the proportion of people in each age group who would say they are vegetarian $(p_{\text{20s}}-p_{\text{30s}})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $99\%$ confidence interval based on Jaime's samples?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Our sample difference

$\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ are the proportions of successes from the two samples. 

In our example $\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ are the proportion of people in each age group who said they were vegetarian. Let's calculate both sample proportions $\hat p_{\text{20s}}$ and $\hat p_{\text{30s}}$ first:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{\text{20s}}&=\dfrac{12}{80}=\blueD{0.15} \\\\
\hat p_{\text{30s}}&=\dfrac{12}{120}=\greenD{0.10}
\end{align}$

So the sample difference is $\hat{p}_{\text{20s}}-\hat{p}_{\text{30s}}=\blueD{0.15}-\greenD{0.10}$.

###Determining the critical value

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $99\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=2.576$.

[[☃ explanation 3]]

##Determining the standard error

The standard error for the difference of two proportions is:

$\text{SE}_{\hat{p}_1-\hat{p}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}_1\left(1-\hat{p}_1\right)}{n_1}+\dfrac{\hat{p}_2\left(1-\hat{p}_2\right)}{n_2}}$

In this example, we use $\hat p_{\text{20s}}=\blueD{0.15}$, $\hat p_{\text{30s}}=\greenD{0.10}$, $n_{\text{20s}}=\purpleD{80}$, and $n_{\text{30s}}=\maroonD{120}$.

$\text{SE}_{\hat{p}_{\text{20s}}-\hat{p}_{\text{30s}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\blueD{0.15}(1-\blueD{0.15})}{\purpleD{80}}+\dfrac{\greenD{0.10}(1-\greenD{0.10})}{\maroonD{120}}}$

###Answer

The correct $99\%$ confidence interval is:

$(0.15-0.10)\pm2.576\sqrt{\dfrac{0.15(0.85)}{80}+\dfrac{0.10(0.90)}{120}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Pharmaceutical scientists tested the effectiveness of a new drug designed to cure malaria. They randomly assigned $400$ infected lab rats to receive either the new drug or an existing drug and recorded how many rats were cured at the end of the study. Here are their results:

 | Cured | Not | Total
- | -: | -: | -: 
New drug | $176$ | $24$ | $200$
Existing drug | $168$ | $32$ | $200$

They want to use these results to construct a $99\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference in the proportion of these rats that would be cured under each drug $(\text{new} - \text{existing})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $99\%$ confidence interval based on their data?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

Our statistic is the difference in the sample proportions $\hat p_{\text{New}}-\hat p_{\text{Existing}}$:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{\text{N}}&=\dfrac{176\text{ cured}}{200 \text{ total}}=\blueD{0.88} \\\\
\hat p_{\text{E}}&=\dfrac{168\text{ cured}}{200 \text{ total}}=\greenD{0.84}
\end{align}$

Our sample sizes are both $n=200$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $99\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=2.576.$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
(\hat p_{\text{N}} -\hat p_{\text{E}}) &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_{\text{N}}(1- \hat p_{\text{N}})}{n_{\text{N}}}+\dfrac{\hat p_{\text{E}}(1- \hat p_{\text{E}})}{n_{\text{E}}}} \\\\
(\blueD{0.88}-\greenD{0.84}) &\pm (2.576) \sqrt{\dfrac{\blueD{0.88}(1-\blueD{0.88})}{200}+\dfrac{\greenD{0.84}(1-\greenD{0.84})}{200}} \\\\
0.04 &\pm (2.576)(0.03464) \\\\
0.04 &\pm 0.089
\end{align}$

###Answer

The correct $99\%$ confidence interval is:

$0.04 \pm 0.089$

assessment_items.question_content:
A sociologist was curious about how much smartphone access has changed for American teenagers. They obtained separate random samples of teenagers from $2017$ and $2013$. Here are their results:

Has access to smartphone? | $2017$ | $2013$
- | -: | -:
Yes | $450$ | $120$
No | $150$ | $280$
**Total** | $600$ | $400$

They want to use these results to construct a $95\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference in the proportion of teens each year who had access to a smartphone $(p_{2017}-p_{2013})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $95\%$ confidence interval based on their data?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

Our statistic is the difference in the sample proportions $\hat p_{2017}-\hat p_{2013}$:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{2017}&=\dfrac{450\text{ have access}}{600 \text{ total}}=\blueD{0.75} \\\\
\hat p_{2013}&=\dfrac{120\text{ have access}}{400 \text{ total}}=\greenD{0.30}
\end{align}$

Our sample sizes are $n_{2017}=\purpleD{600}$ and $n_{2013}=\maroonD{400}$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96.$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Answer

The correct $95\%$ confidence interval is:

$0.45 \pm 0.057$

assessment_items.question_content:
Marta created an app, and she suspected that teens were more likely to use it than adults. She obtained separate random samples of teens and adults. Here are the results:

Use app? | Teens | Adults
- | -: | -:
Yes | $54$ | $26$
No | $66$ | $104$
**Total** | $120$ | $130$

She wants to use these results to construct a $95\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference between the proportions of teens and adults that use her app $(p_\text{T}-p_\text{A})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $95\%$ confidence interval based on her data?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

Our statistic is the difference in the sample proportions $\hat p_{\text{T}}-\hat p_{\text{A}}$:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{\text{T}}&=\dfrac{54\text{ use app}}{120 \text{ total}}=\blueD{0.45} \\\\
\hat p_{\text{A}}&=\dfrac{26\text{ use app}}{130 \text{ total}}=\greenD{0.20}
\end{align}$

Our sample sizes are $n_{\text{T}}=\purpleD{120}$ and $n_{\text{A}}=\maroonD{130}$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96.$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Computation

$\begin{align}
(\hat p_{\text{T}} -\hat p_{\text{A}}) &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p_{\text{T}}(1- \hat p_{\text{T}})}{n_{\text{T}}}+\dfrac{\hat p_{\text{A}}(1- \hat p_{\text{A}})}{n_{\text{A}}}} \\\\
(\blueD{0.45}-\greenD{0.20}) &\pm (1.96) \sqrt{\dfrac{\blueD{0.45}(1-\blueD{0.45})}{\purpleD{120}}+\dfrac{\greenD{0.20}(1-\greenD{0.20})}{\maroonD{130}}} \\\\
0.25 &\pm (1.96)(0.0574) \\\\
0.25 &\pm 0.112479
\end{align}$

###Answer

The correct $95\%$ confidence interval is:

$0.25 \pm 0.112$

assessment_items.question_content:
Schools in a large county are categorized into one of two regions: East or West. Administrators want to estimate the difference in the proportions of students participating in Advanced Placement programs in each region. They take a random sample of $50$ East students and find that $20$ of them have taken at least one AP class. A separate random sample of $80$ West students shows $16$ students that have taken at least one AP class.

They want to use these results to construct a $95\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference in the proportion of students taking an AP class in each region $(\text{East} - \text{West})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $95\%$ confidence interval based on their data?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

Our statistic is the difference in the sample proportions $\hat p_{\text{East}}-\hat p_{\text{West}}$:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{\text{E}}&=\dfrac{20\text{ took AP}}{50 \text{ total}}=\blueD{0.40} \\\\
\hat p_{\text{W}}&=\dfrac{16\text{ took AP}}{80 \text{ total}}=\greenD{0.20}
\end{align}$

Our sample sizes are $n_\text{E}=\purpleD{50}$ and $n_\text{W}=\maroonD{80}$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96.$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

The correct $95\%$ confidence interval is:

$(0.038,0.362)$

assessment_items.question_content:
Duncan is investigating if residents of a city support the construction of a new high school. He's curious about the difference of opinion between residents in the North and South parts of the city. He obtained separate random samples of residents from each region. Here are the results:

Supports construction? | North | South
- | -: | -:
Yes | $54$ | $77$
No | $66$ | $63$
**Total** | $120$ | $140$

Duncan wants to use these results to construct a $90\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference in the proportion of residents in these regions who support the construction project $(p_\text{S}-p_\text{N})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $90\%$ confidence interval based on Duncan's samples?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

Our statistic is the difference in the sample proportions $\hat p_{\text{South}}-\hat p_{\text{North}}$:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{\text{S}}&=\dfrac{77\text{ support}}{140 \text{ total}}=\blueD{0.55} \\\\
\hat p_{\text{N}}&=\dfrac{54\text{ support}}{120 \text{ total}}=\greenD{0.45}
\end{align}$

Our sample sizes are $n_\text{S}=\purpleD{140}$ and $n_\text{N}=\maroonD{120}$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $90\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.645.$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

The correct $90\%$ confidence interval is:

$(-0.002,0.202)$

assessment_items.question_content:
A member of congress is curious how voters in two major political parties support a certain policy change. They obtained separate random samples of voters from each party. Here are the results:

Supports policy change? | Democrat | Republican
- | -: | -:
Yes | $80$ | $150$
No | $320$ | $450$
**Total** | $400$ | $600$

They want to use these results to construct a $99\%$ confidence interval to estimate the difference in the proportion of voters in these parties who support the policy change $(p_\text{R}-p_\text{D})$. Assume that all of the conditions for inference have been met.

**Which of the following is a correct $99\%$ confidence interval based on these samples?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Components of formula

Our statistic is the difference in the sample proportions $\hat p_{\text{Rep}}-\hat p_{\text{Dem}}$:

$\begin{align}
\hat p_{\text{R}}&=\dfrac{150\text{ support}}{600 \text{ total}}=\blueD{0.25} \\\\
\hat p_{\text{D}}&=\dfrac{80\text{ support}}{400 \text{ total}}=\greenD{0.20}
\end{align}$

Our sample sizes are $n_\text{R}=\purpleD{600}$ and $n_\text{D}=\maroonD{400}$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $99\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=2.576.$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Answer

The correct $99\%$ confidence interval is:

$(-0.019,0.119)$
